metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC (e.g. `git -C <worktree> log origin/tickets --oneline -1 | grep code_review` for AC4, `git config credential.helper` + `env | grep -v REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` for AC2, `grep -r 'material_fingerprint\|norm_id' <new_file>` pointing at the reused import for AC3).
findings.finding: No AC has a concrete proving command — zero `git`, `jq`, `grep`, or shell commands are given anywhere in the plan to produce evidence that any criterion is satisfied.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
findings.suggested_fix:
Append a 'Verification' block after the ACs with one shell command per AC:
```
# AC1 – type is registered and excluded from dep-graph / Jira sync
rebar query --type code_review --limit 1 | jq -e '.type == "code_review"'

# AC2 – credential helper in effect, PAT not in remote URL
git -C <tickets-worktree> remote get-url origin          # must NOT contain a token
git -C <tickets-worktree> config credential.helper       # must echo the helper

# AC3 – no new fingerprint algorithm (reuse verified by import)
grep -rn 'material_fingerprint\|norm_id' src/reviewbot/ | grep -v 'plan_review'

# AC4 – artifact lands on origin/tickets after a synthetic review
git -C <tickets-worktree> fetch origin tickets
git -C <tickets-worktree> log origin/tickets --oneline -5 | grep code_review
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or expand AC4) with a runnable end-to-end step that triggers a synthetic review in the reviewbot container and then asserts `git -C <worktree> fetch && git log origin/tickets --oneline | grep -q code_review` against the real origin, not a mock.
findings.finding: The end-to-end push path (reviewbot → PAT-credentialed push → origin/tickets) is the single riskiest new behavior, but AC4 contains no criterion that exercises it against the live origin — 'on a real review the artifact is written and lands on origin/tickets' is a prose assertion, not a runnable check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4; What / STORE bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default artifact payload schema (at minimum: field names for impact, tier tags, norm_id, material_fingerprint, relates_to) or explicitly cite the existing plan_review schema as the template.
findings.finding: The artifact payload schema ('impact + tier-tagged sub-answers') is described in prose but the specific field names are not enumerated; an executor must infer them from the existing plan_review artifact, creating an ambiguity that could yield inconsistent field naming.
findings.location: What section, first paragraph ('Emit a change-scoped code_review artifact … carrying the impact + tier-tagged sub-answers + norm_id/material_fingerprint join keys')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-liner: 'Artifact fields mirror the plan_review artifact schema (see `rebar/artifacts/plan_review.py`); reuse field names verbatim: `impact`, `tier_tags`, `norm_id`, `material_fingerprint`, `relates_to`.''

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the SSM parameter path (e.g. `/prod/reviewbot/REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT`) in the plan so the executor does not have to choose or discover it.
findings.finding: The SSM parameter name/path for REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT is not stated; an executor must choose or discover it, which is a real operational decision (IAM policy, parameter path convention) that should be a plan-level commitment, not an executor choice.
findings.location: Scope bullet ('an SSM materialize step for the PAT'); What / CREDENTIAL bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the CREDENTIAL bullet: 'SSM parameter path: `/prod/reviewbot/REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` (create if absent, SecureString, same KMS key as other reviewbot secrets).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or extend AC #1 to verify that a written `code_review` artifact contains the required payload fields: `impact`, tier-tagged sub-answers, `norm_id`, and `material_fingerprint`.
findings.finding: The artifact content fields described in What — 'impact + tier-tagged sub-answers' — have no covering acceptance criterion; AC #1 verifies only the artifact key, graph exclusion, Jira-sync exclusion, and searchability, not the required payload fields.
findings.location: What section ('carrying the impact + tier-tagged sub-answers + norm_id/material_fingerprint join keys'); AC #1
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC #1: 'The stored artifact contains: `impact` score, tier-tagged sub-answers, `norm_id`, `material_fingerprint`, and `relates_to` links — verifiable by inspecting a committed artifact JSON in the tickets branch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the prior state of `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH` in the reviewbot environment, confirm the setting is scoped to the code_review emission path (or justify global scope), and add a rollback step (e.g. revert the env var, or a feature flag) if push-always causes conflicts.
findings.finding: `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always` is introduced as a new environment variable for the reviewbot, but the plan does not state its previous value or acknowledge what happens to any existing rebar sync behavior if this setting changes; no rollback path is provided if enabling it causes unexpected pushes or conflicts on the tickets branch.
findings.location: What section ('REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always'); Scope ('persistent store')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry/backoff behavior for the git push to origin/tickets and define what happens to the local worktree (and the review run) when all retries fail — at minimum log a structured error and surface it as a non-zero exit or metric.
findings.finding: The git push to origin/tickets is an external write with no retry, backoff, or circuit-breaker specified; if the push fails (network blip, GitHub API outage), the plan has no stated recovery path, leaving the local worktree in an unpushed/diverged state with no operator signal.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — STORE bullet; AC line 2 ('lands on origin/tickets')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a push-retry loop (e.g., 3 attempts with exponential backoff); on final failure, log a structured WARNING with the artifact key and skip the push without failing the review gate, so the review output is not blocked. On next successful run, a `git push --force-with-lease` from the same worktree will recover.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add structured log events (and ideally a metric counter) for: (1) successful push to origin/tickets, (2) push failure with artifact key, (3) SSM PAT materialization failure at startup — so operators can alert on push failure rate.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for the new failure points: git push failures, SSM PAT materialization failures, or worktree initialization errors — operators have no metric, log, or alert to detect when artifacts are not reaching origin/tickets.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — CREDENTIAL and STORE bullets; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define degraded-mode behavior: if SSM materialization fails, should the reviewbot start without push capability (warn + skip push) or refuse to start? If all push retries fail, should the review gate still emit a result? Make the push path non-blocking for the review output.
findings.finding: The plan does not address degraded-mode behavior when hard external dependencies (GitHub/origin or SSM) are unavailable: if SSM is down at container startup the PAT cannot be materialized; if GitHub is down pushes always fail — it is unspecified whether the review gate itself is blocked or degrades gracefully.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — CREDENTIAL bullet; Scope ('SSM materialize step')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the code_review artifact type's semantic contract (session_log pattern, exclusion from dependency graph and Jira sync, join-key schema) and the reviewbot credential-helper pattern, so future contributors have a canonical reference.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel cross-component architectural pattern (reviewbot as a ticket-store writer, persistent worktree in the review container, new code_review artifact type with session_log-style semantics) with no corresponding ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc reference.
findings.location: Scope section; What section ('session_log pattern'); AC line 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how concurrent worktree write+push operations are serialized (e.g., advisory file lock around the git-add/commit/push sequence, or a per-worktree queue) and define the retry/rebase strategy when a push is rejected as non-fast-forward.
findings.finding: The persistent tickets worktree is a shared mutable filesystem resource, but the plan does not specify how concurrent code-review runs (parallel Gerrit events processed simultaneously by the reviewbot) are serialized when writing to and pushing from the same worktree. Two parallel pushes will produce a rejected non-fast-forward push for the second writer, with no retry/rebase strategy described.
findings.location: ## What — STORE bullet; AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the worktree write+push in an exclusive flock (or equivalent) per worktree path. On push rejection (exit 1 from git push), do a git pull --rebase and retry once before logging a non-fatal error. Document this in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define who initializes the persistent worktree (e.g., a container entrypoint script that clones if the path is absent), what path it lives on, and what the emit code does if initialization has not yet completed.
findings.finding: The plan says the persistent worktree is 'materialized once' but does not specify who triggers that initialization, when (first container start, a separate bootstrap job, a CI step), or what happens if the worktree directory is absent when the first review event fires.
findings.location: ## What — STORE bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Dockerfile/entrypoint step: `if [ ! -d $TICKETS_WORKTREE/.git ]; then git clone --branch tickets $TICKETS_REMOTE $TICKETS_WORKTREE; fi`. Document this in the Scope section and add an AC line covering the absent-worktree case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure (at minimum: what to do if SSM secret is absent at startup, how to disable artifact emission without reverting the image, and whether already-pushed worktree commits need cleanup), and specify that the SSM secret must be provisioned before the container image is deployed.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a behavior change to the deployed reviewbot (new artifact emission, new credential helper, new SSM secret dependency) with no feature flag, no staged rollout, and no explicit rollback procedure. In particular, SSM secret absence at container start is not handled, and reverting the code does not undo already-pushed worktree commits if the change needs to be pulled back.
findings.location: ## Scope; AC lines 1–4
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a `REBAR_CODE_REVIEW_EMIT=true/false` env var (defaulting false on first deploy, flipped to true after the worktree and secret are confirmed working) to gate the new emission path. Document: 'to roll back, set REBAR_CODE_REVIEW_EMIT=false and redeploy; already-pushed artifacts are retained intentionally.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicit non-fatal fallback spec) for push/credential failure: define observable behavior when the PAT is absent from SSM, when git push is rejected, or when the network is unreachable (e.g., log error + continue review, emit metric, or surface in reviewbot output) so the live boundary has a tested failure path.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a live external boundary (git push via PAT-echoing credential helper with REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=always) but states no acceptance criterion or test case for the failure path: PAT absent from SSM, PAT expired/revoked, push rejected by origin, or network timeout. The only failure path covered (unresolved trailer refs) is unrelated to the push/auth layer. A broken PAT or unreachable origin would silently drop the artifact or crash the reviewbot with no specified handling.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #2 (credential helper) and AC #4 (artifact lands on origin/tickets)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC #2: 'If REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT is absent or the push fails, the reviewbot logs a structured error at ERROR level and continues (non-fatal); a canary alert fires on consecutive push failures.' Add a corresponding integration test step that temporarily unsets the PAT and asserts the reviewbot completes the review and logs the expected error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify exactly what input `material_fingerprint` receives for code-review artifacts (the raw diff? a sub-finding dict? a derived context struct?) and confirm it is shape-compatible with the existing `PlanContext`-taking function — or explicitly name the adaptation needed to avoid forking the algorithm.
findings.finding: `material_fingerprint` is defined over `PlanContext` (plan text + AC fields), not a git diff; applying it unchanged to a code-review finding/diff forces a plan-review abstraction onto a structurally different input type. The plan claims 'no new algorithm' but the adaptation needed to make `material_fingerprint(ctx)` meaningful over a diff may be non-trivial or produce a semantically empty fingerprint.
findings.location: What section — 'JOIN KEYS: norm_id and material_fingerprint are computed by the EXISTING plan-review sidecar machinery … applied to the code-review finding/diff — no new algorithm.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` to `fetch-secrets.sh` (new `get_param` call + `.env` line), provision the corresponding `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` SSM SecureString parameter, and confirm the EC2 instance-role IAM policy grants `ssm:GetParameter` + `kms:Decrypt` for this new path — none of these currently exist.
findings.finding: `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` is asserted to be 'SSM-materialized' in the reviewbot's `.env`, but `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` — the sole SSM-to-.env materializer — contains no entry for this key and no corresponding SSM parameter name. The plan's credential mechanism rests entirely on this assertion, yet it does not exist anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: What / CREDENTIAL section: 'the reviewbot reads the PAT from its SSM-materialized `.env` (`REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In fetch-secrets.sh, add `reviewbot_tickets_pat="$(get_param reviewbot-tickets-pat)"` alongside the other required params, and emit `echo "REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT=${reviewbot_tickets_pat}"` in the .env block. In infra/terraform/ssm.tf (or equivalent), provision the `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` SecureString. Update iam_s2.tf to include the new SSM path in the GetParameter allow-list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` and `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` to the Scope/edit-set, explicitly declaring the new `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` SSM slot and its mapping to `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` in the `.env`.
findings.finding: `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` does not exist anywhere in the codebase: it is absent from `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` (the SSM→.env mapper), from `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` (the SSM slot registry), and from the reviewbot container's env. The plan names it as the credential source but the SSM slot, the Terraform resource, and the fetch-secrets mapping are all new edits — none of which appear in the plan's Scope or edit-set.
findings.location: What section (CREDENTIAL bullet) + Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` (new `"/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat"` entry in `rebar_secret_params`), and `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` (new `reviewbot_tickets_pat="$(get_param reviewbot-tickets-pat)"` call and a corresponding `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT=...` line in the `.env` write block).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py` (updating `EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES`) to the plan's Scope/edit-set as a required consumer update when introducing the `code_review` ticket type.
findings.finding: Adding a new `code_review` ticket type requires updating `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py`'s `EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES` frozenset (to exclude `code_review` from Jira sync) and verifying `outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` lacks a `code_review` entry — these are direct unenumerated consumers outside the emission code. The plan's Scope does not list these reconciler files.
findings.location: Scope section; AC line 1 ('excluded from Jira sync')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py` — add `"code_review"` to `EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES`. Mirror the pattern from the `session_log` addition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the new persistent volume for the tickets worktree (host path + compose volume declaration + `compose-up.sh` creation step) in the Scope/edit-set as a concrete `docker-compose.yml` edit.
findings.finding: The persistent tickets worktree inside the reviewbot container requires a new volume/bind-mount in `docker-compose.yml`, but the plan does not name this as an edit to `docker-compose.yml` beyond generic 'reviewbot infra'. The existing `gerrit_reviewbot` volume only covers the dedup ledger path (`/var/gerrit/site/reviewbot`). A git worktree at a different path (e.g., `/var/gerrit/site/tickets-store`) requires its own persistent volume declaration and a `compose-up.sh` creation step — neither is named in the Scope.
findings.location: Scope section (STORE bullet in What; 'docker-compose.yml' in Scope)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly what object/fields are passed to `material_fingerprint` for a code-review context (e.g. map `diff_text` → `description`, `change_id` → `ticket_id`, empty lists for `file_impact`/`children`), or acknowledge that a thin wrapper/adapter is needed and describe its field mapping.
findings.finding: The plan claims to reuse the existing `material_fingerprint` function 'applied to the code-review finding/diff', but the function signature in `pass1.py:496-507` takes a `PlanContext` object (fields: `ticket_id`, `description`, `file_impact`, `children`) — none of which exist in a code-review context. Calling it on a diff requires constructing a synthetic `PlanContext`-shaped object whose field mapping is entirely unspecified by the plan. The 'no new algorithm' claim is technically true for the hash, but the input-object construction IS new, non-trivial logic, and its omission means the implementer will have to invent the adaptation.
findings.location: What section — 'JOIN KEYS' bullet; AC #3 ('norm_id/material_fingerprint reuse the existing rebar fingerprinting functions')
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'JOIN KEYS' bullet, add one sentence: 'For `material_fingerprint`, construct a minimal dict/adapter with `ticket_id=<gerrit-change-id>`, `description=<diff_text[:N]>`, `file_impact=<changed_files list>`, `children=[]` — this is the only new code; the hash function itself is unchanged.' This preserves the 'no new algorithm' claim while making the adaptation explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly compare the proposed `code_review` ticket-type artifact against the existing `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` `REVIEW_RESULT` emission path and justify (in the plan) why a new artifact type is needed rather than extending the existing sidecar with the change-key, `norm_id`, and `material_fingerprint` fields.
findings.finding: The codebase already contains `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`, which emits `REVIEW_RESULT` events on target tickets — an existing mechanism to durably write code-review results to the rebar ticket store. The plan proposes an entirely new `code_review` artifact type (a first-class ticket, session_log-style) without explaining why the existing sidecar emission path is insufficient for the stated goals (durable record + corpus for impact-model calibration). This risks reinventing/repackaging prior art that already exists in the repo.
findings.location: What section (mechanism description) / src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Cite the canary run artifact (ticket ID, log link, or run timestamp) that confirms PAT push from the reviewbot container, and confirm the SSM parameter path (e.g. `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat`) already exists in the AWS account or add its provisioning to the Scope.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that 'the RebarBotNava PAT push capability is already confirmed working from the reviewbot container' and calls the credential-helper mechanism 'the exact mechanism validated by the on-box canary' — but provides no pointer to any verifiable evidence (no log link, no ticket, no canary run output, no SSM parameter path). Separately, `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` (the existing SSM materialization script) does not include `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT`, leaving the SSM parameter's existence unconfirmed.
findings.location: Why section ('RebarBotNava PAT push capability is already confirmed') / What section (CREDENTIAL mechanism) / infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Provision SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat`' and extend `fetch-secrets.sh` to read it; link the canary run ticket in the Why section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT is a fine-grained GitHub PAT (or GitHub App installation token) scoped to `contents: write` on the tickets repository only — document this scope constraint in the plan's CREDENTIAL section.
findings.finding: The plan never scopes the REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT's GitHub permissions. A classic PAT with broad repo access (e.g., full `repo` scope) is a long-lived credential with write access across all repos the token owner can reach, violating least-privilege. The plan should specify a fine-grained PAT restricted to contents:write on the single tickets repository.
findings.location: What / CREDENTIAL bullet; Scope / SSM materialize step
findings.suggested_fix: Use a fine-grained PAT restricted to the single `tickets` repository with only `contents: write` permission, or prefer a GitHub App installation token with an explicit repo+permission binding. Add one sentence to the CREDENTIAL bullet: 'The PAT is a fine-grained token with `contents: write` on the `tickets` repo only; no other repo or org permission is granted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cost/sizing note to the plan acknowledging expected artifact volume and either a growth bound, a future review trigger (e.g., 'reassess when repo exceeds X GB'), or a shallow-worktree strategy to avoid full-history clones.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly designates 'No retention cap (deliberate)' for the code_review artifact store without acknowledging unbounded git repository growth as a cost or operational concern. A perpetually growing git history with binary/diff payloads per review will increase clone time, storage cost, and push latency over time.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last AC bullet: 'No retention cap (deliberate)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the idempotency guard for worktree materialization (e.g., 'skip clone if worktree directory is already a valid git repo; run `git fetch && git reset --hard origin/tickets` to reconcile') in the STORE bullet.
findings.finding: The plan says the persistent tickets worktree is 'materialized once (clone of the `tickets` branch)' but does not specify the idempotency guard for subsequent container restarts or redeployments. If the worktree directory already exists, a naive clone will fail or produce a stale state.
findings.location: What / STORE bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the STORE bullet: 'Materialization script checks for an existing `.git` directory; if present, runs `git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/tickets` instead of cloning. This makes the step idempotent across container restarts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability note: specify how push failures are surfaced (e.g., logged to stderr with a non-zero exit that surfaces in container logs, or a counter/alert), so operators know when the persistent store is not receiving artifacts.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability (logging, alerting, or failure signalling) for the persistent store push path. A failed `git push` (e.g., merge conflict on the tickets branch, expired PAT, network partition) will silently drop the code_review artifact with no operator notification.
findings.location: What / STORE bullet; Acceptance Criteria overall

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a teardown/recovery note to the Scope or STORE section: document that the worktree is fully reconstructible via a fresh clone of `origin/tickets` and that no local state is canonical (canonical state lives on the remote).
findings.finding: The plan has no teardown or reproducibility procedure for the persistent tickets worktree infra. Without documented teardown steps, the worktree on its Docker volume becomes an unmanaged, unrecoverable artifact if the host is replaced or the volume is lost.
findings.location: Scope section; What / STORE bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The local worktree is non-canonical; canonical state is `origin/tickets`. Recovery from volume loss is a fresh clone — no backup of the local worktree is required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` to `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` (fetch + write) and add the `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat` SecureString placeholder to `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`; confirm both are listed in the Scope section.
findings.finding: `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` SSM materialization is asserted but contradicted by both infra files that implement the SSM→.env pipeline. `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` fetches exactly six named params (anthropic-api-key, mcp-hmac-signing-key, gerrit-admin-password, gerrit-bot-token, github-oauth-client-id, github-oauth-client-secret) and writes no `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` line. `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` provisions parameter slots for a fixed list that contains no reviewbot-tickets-pat entry. The plan's entire SSM-materialized-credential path depends on both of these files being updated, but neither is mentioned in Scope.
findings.location: What > CREDENTIAL section; Scope bullet 1 ('SSM materialize step for the PAT')
findings.suggested_fix: In fetch-secrets.sh: add `reviewbot_tickets_pat="$(get_param reviewbot-tickets-pat)"` in the fetch block and `echo "REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT=${reviewbot_tickets_pat}"` in the .env write block. In ssm.tf: add `"/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-pat"` to `rebar_secret_params`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `"code_review"` to the `ticket_type` enum in `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json` and to `EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES` in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py`; list these registration points explicitly in the Scope section.
findings.finding: `code_review` does not exist as a ticket type and its creation is blocked by a strict enum. `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json` defines `ticket_type` as a string enum: `["bug", "epic", "story", "task", "session_log"]`. Any CREATE event with `ticket_type: code_review` will fail schema validation. Additionally, `EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES` in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py` only contains `session_log`; `code_review` must be added there, and potentially to `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` absence checks. The plan's AC1 asserts this type 'exists' but provides no detail on where to register it.
findings.location: AC #1: 'A `code_review` artifact type/handling exists'; What > 'Emit a change-scoped `code_review` artifact'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the plan how the code-review diff/finding is projected into the `norm_id(finding: dict)` and `material_fingerprint(ctx: PlanContext)` input shapes, OR acknowledge that a thin adapter (the 'no new algorithm' claim needs scoping) is required.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `norm_id` and `material_fingerprint` are reusable with 'no new algorithm', but both functions require input shapes specific to plan-review that a code-review diff/finding does not naturally provide. `norm_id(finding)` expects a dict with `finding` (prose text) and `criteria` (list of criterion IDs). `material_fingerprint(ctx)` takes a `PlanContext` with `ticket_id`, `description`, `file_impact`, and `children`. A Gerrit code-review finding/diff is neither shape; adapting the diff into these shapes requires new mapping logic at the call site. The plan does not describe how the diff/finding is projected into these input shapes.
findings.location: What > JOIN KEYS section: 'no new algorithm'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture of the `origin/tickets` remote (public-with-rationale / private / internal-only) and document who holds read access, so the boundary is explicit and reviewable (T5c: undeclared access level on boundary-crossing data path).
findings.finding: The access posture of the `tickets` branch (the remote `origin/tickets`) is never declared — public, private, or internal-only. Code-review artifacts carrying impact scores, tier-tagged findings, and diff-derived fingerprints are pushed there; if the repo is public or broadly readable the artifacts are exposed to any GitHub user with repo read access.
findings.location: What section — 'STORE: a persistent tickets worktree…auto-pushes to origin' / AC: 'lands on origin/tickets'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line statement in the Scope or What section: e.g., 'The tickets repo is private to the org; read access is restricted to [team/role].' If the repo is public, explicitly acknowledge that artifact content is public and confirm no sensitive diff material is included.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the minimum required GitHub OAuth scopes for `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` (e.g., `contents:write` scoped to the tickets repo) and note whether a fine-grained PAT (repo-scoped) is used instead of a classic PAT (T5c: least-privilege on boundary-crossing credential).
findings.finding: The `REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT` GitHub Personal Access Token's required OAuth scopes are never declared. Without a scope declaration, the token may be provisioned with broad permissions (e.g., full `repo` scope) rather than the minimum required for pushing to a single branch.
findings.location: What section — 'CREDENTIAL: the reviewbot reads the PAT from its SSM-materialized .env (REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a GitHub fine-grained PAT scoped to only the target repository with 'Contents: Read and write' permission. Add to the Scope or Credential section: 'Fine-grained PAT with Contents:write on <repo> only; no other scopes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the contradictory 'never on disk' claim: specify the `.env` file's filesystem location, permissions (e.g., 0600, readable only by the reviewbot process user), mount type (tmpfs preferred), and whether it is purged after the credential helper is configured — so the secret lifecycle on the boundary-crossing path is fully declared (T5c: secret lifecycle on boundary-crossing path).
findings.finding: The plan simultaneously claims the PAT is 'never written to…disk' and that it is 'SSM-materialized' to a `.env` file — these are contradictory. The `.env` file IS a file on disk; its permissions, filesystem location, mount lifecycle (ephemeral tmpfs vs. persistent volume), and who can read it inside the container are unspecified, leaving the secret-lifecycle contract on the boundary-crossing path incomplete.
findings.location: What section — 'the reviewbot reads the PAT from its SSM-materialized .env (REVIEWBOT_TICKETS_PAT)' / 'the token is never written to the remote URL or disk'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the token is never written to the remote URL or disk' with an accurate description such as: 'The PAT is materialized to a tmpfs-mounted .env (chmod 0600, owned by the reviewbot service user) and is not written into the git remote URL or committed to any repo. The file is purged after the credential helper is registered.' This closes the lifecycle gap and removes the false claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full field-level schema for the `code_review` artifact (field names, types, required/optional) including how `impact`, `tier-tagged sub-answers`, `norm_id`, and `material_fingerprint` are serialized — either inline in What or as a referenced schema file.
findings.finding: The `code_review` artifact schema is referenced but never defined: the plan names 'impact + tier-tagged sub-answers + norm_id/material_fingerprint' as artifact fields but provides no field-level schema (types, required/optional, serialization format). An executor cannot write the emit code or the AC#1 'type/handling' without this vocabulary.
findings.location: What section — 'Emit a change-scoped `code_review` artifact keyed by (gerrit-change-id, revision), carrying the impact + tier-tagged sub-answers…'; AC#1 — 'A `code_review` artifact type/handling exists'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete dict shape passed to `plan_review.sidecar.norm_id` in the code-review context: which field(s) of the code-review output constitute the `finding` text and `criteria` list inputs, or acknowledge that an adapter is needed.
findings.finding: The `norm_id` reuse protocol is under-specified: `plan_review.sidecar.norm_id(finding)` takes a dict with `finding` (text string) and `criteria` (list of criterion IDs). The plan says to apply this 'to the code-review finding/diff' but never states what dict is passed — which fields of the code-review output constitute the `finding` text and `criteria` inputs.
findings.location: What section — 'JOIN KEYS: `norm_id` and `material_fingerprint` are computed by the EXISTING plan-review sidecar machinery (reuse `plan_review.sidecar`/finder `norm_id` fingerprinting…) applied to the code-review finding/diff'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify exactly how `material_fingerprint` is applied in the code-review context: either specify the `PlanContext`-field ↔ diff mapping, or acknowledge that a new (but analogous) hash function is needed and define its input fields.
findings.finding: `material_fingerprint` is defined over `PlanContext` (a structured object with `ticket_id`, `description`, `file_impact`, `children` fields) but the plan says to apply it to 'the code-review finding/diff — no new algorithm'. A git diff is not a `PlanContext`; the adaptation protocol (what becomes `ticket_id`, `description`, etc.) is unspecified and contradicts 'no new algorithm'.
findings.location: What section — 'JOIN KEYS: `norm_id` and `material_fingerprint` are computed by the EXISTING plan-review sidecar machinery … applied to the code-review finding/diff — no new algorithm'; AC#3 — '`norm_id`/`material_fingerprint` reuse the existing rebar fingerprinting functions (no new algorithm)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the concrete implementation touchpoints required to register `code_review` with the session_log pattern: `EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES`, graph-exclusion predicate, gate-exemption path, and any ticket-type registration — do not leave these implicit in the analogy.
findings.finding: The `code_review` artifact type lifecycle rules are referenced by analogy to `session_log` but never enumerated: AC#1 says 'kept OUT of the dependency graph, excluded from Jira sync, searchable (session_log-style)' but does not specify which concrete mechanisms implement this — i.e., which config constant (e.g., `EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES`), which graph-exclusion predicate, which gate-exemption path, and which ticket-type registration entry to add or modify.
findings.location: AC#1 — 'A `code_review` artifact type/handling exists: keyed by change+revision, kept OUT of the dependency graph, excluded from Jira sync, searchable (session_log-style)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Touchpoints' sub-list to AC#1 mirroring the session_log pattern: (1) add `'code_review'` to `EXCLUDED_SYNC_TYPES` in `config.py`; (2) update the graph-exclusion predicate; (3) confirm gate-exemption; (4) add ticket-type registration entry.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
